file_name: Mandal et al. - 2024 - Active learning with particle swarm optimization for enhanced skin cancer classification utilizing d.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Active Learning (AL) combined with Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to select informative data points for training Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models (specifically EfficientNetV2M) for skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' in the header. It presents a novel methodology (AL-PSO), describes experimental setups using the HAM10000 dataset, reports original quantitative results (e.g., 89.44% accuracy with EfficientNetV2M), and includes sections for Introduction, Methodology, Experimental Setups, Results, and Conclusion, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Active Learning with Particle Swarm Optimization for Enhanced Skin Cancer Classification Utilizing Deep CNN Models
journal: Journal of Imaging Informatics in Medicine
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer classification by proposing an active learning framework integrated with particle swarm optimization (AL-PSO) to reduce the reliance on large annotated datasets. The authors applied this method to the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset, utilizing data augmentation to balance classes and training six pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network models via transfer learning. The AL-PSO strategy selectively identified the most informative data points, allowing models to be trained on only 35% to 40% of the labeled data. Experimental results demonstrated that the EfficientNetV2M model, combined with the Least Confidence PSO strategy, achieved the highest classification accuracy of 89.44%. This performance significantly outperformed traditional transfer learning and standalone active learning approaches, highlighting the method's efficiency and potential for clinical adoption.

all_task_types.target: Seven categories of skin lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) from the HAM10000 dataset
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on skin cancer classification using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) like EfficientNetV2M. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant or specific lesion types) from dermatoscopic images. The primary metric reported is classification accuracy (89.44%), along with precision, recall, and F1-score. The study utilizes active learning strategies (Least Confidence, Highest Margin, etc.) to select data points for training the classifier, but the final model output is a class label, not a continuous value.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive collection of 10,015 dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, sourced from Professor Cliff Rosendahl’s clinical practice in Queensland, Australia, and the Dermatology Department of the Medical University of Vienna, Austria. It is the same dataset used for the ISIC 2018 challenge.
classes_used: akiec (Actinic keratosis and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease)

use.training: Used as the primary dataset. Initially, 80% (8,012 images) was allocated for training and validation. After data augmentation to balance classes, the augmented dataset (33,789 images) served as the unlabeled pool for active learning. A subset of 10% (1,001 images) was initially used for preliminary training, and the remaining 90% was used as an unlabeled pool for AL-PSO selection. The final models were trained on 35% to 40% of the labeled data selected via AL-PSO.
use.validation: 10% of the initial training/validation split (801 images) was designated as the validation set.
use.testing: 20% of the original dataset (2,003 images) was reserved for testing.
use.other: The augmented dataset (33,789 images) was designated as the unlabeled pool for the active learning process.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compute the consistency and variability of accuracy values over all epochs for training and validation phases, and to ensure robustness of metrics across multiple runs.

To assess statistical significance of differences in validation loss and accuracy between models and strategies (e.g., comparing EfficientNetV2M to other models, Entropy-based method to Lowest Margin method).
To evaluate class-wise performance, specifically identifying false positive and false negative error rates for different classes and strategies.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in validation loss or accuracy between the compared models or strategies (e.g., EfficientNetV2M vs. other models, Entropy-based method vs. Lowest Margin method).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in validation loss or accuracy between the compared models or strategies.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper reports p-values to assess statistical significance. Specifically, it states that the lower mean validation loss of EfficientNetV2M is statistically supported with p < 0.05 compared to other models. It also reports a statistically significant mean difference in validation accuracy between the Entropy-based method and the Lowest Margin method with p < 0.05, and a significant difference in validation accuracy for EfficientNetV2M over other models with p < 0.01.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed approach obtained the highest accuracy among compared methods.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ResNet50 extractor with MLP classifier and active learning
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to address class imbalance: random rotations (up to 180 degrees), horizontal and vertical flips, resizing, cropping, adjustments in hue and saturation, and application of Gaussian blur.
Resizing: All images in the final training dataset were resized to a resolution of 255 × 255 pixels.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to minority class images, with rotations up to 180 degrees.
Applied to minority class images to increase their representation.
All images in the final training dataset were resized to a resolution of 255 × 255 pixels.

conclusions: The proposed Active Learning with Particle Swarm Optimization (AL-PSO) framework significantly improves skin cancer classification accuracy while reducing the reliance on large annotated datasets, requiring only 35% to 40% of labeled data.

The EfficientNetV2M model combined with the Least Confidence PSO (LC-PSO) strategy achieved the highest classification accuracy of 89.44%, outperforming traditional transfer learning and standalone active learning approaches.

The LC-PSO strategy provided a balanced performance by efficiently addressing both false positive and false negative errors, whereas other strategies like Lowest Margin performed poorly and Entropy-based methods struggled with false negatives.
The integration of AL and PSO accelerates the adoption of AI in clinical settings for skin cancer detection by minimizing labeling costs and optimizing classifier performance.
The proposed method is adaptable and can be extended to other medical image classification tasks that face challenges with imbalanced datasets and limited labeled data.
The code for the study is publicly available to support reproducibility and further research.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is sourced from two specific locations (Queensland, Australia and Vienna, Austria), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Significant class imbalance was addressed via aggressive data augmentation (up to 4827 images per class), which may introduce synthetic bias and overestimate performance on real-world imbalanced data.

The model exhibited notable difficulty in distinguishing Melanoma (mel) from Melanocytic Nevus (nv), with a significant number of melanoma samples misclassified as nevi, posing a critical clinical risk for false negatives.

The Lowest Margin strategy performed poorly across all metrics with statistical significance (p < 0.05), indicating that not all active learning strategies are equally effective when combined with PSO.
The paper reports accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score but does not provide confidence intervals for the reported metrics, limiting the assessment of statistical robustness beyond p-values.
The code for the study is publicly available on GitHub, supporting reproducibility.
The proposed AL-PSO method achieved 89.44% accuracy using only 35-40% of labeled data, significantly outperforming traditional transfer learning and standalone active learning approaches.
